english: fried eggplant with tomato sauce Turkish style

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Saksuka. The closest defensible parent is 'vegetable dish' (FOODON_03302184). A more specific Turkish meze term is not available in FoodOn as of April 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Saksuka. A00LC (Cooked vegetables, mixed) is the closest defensible parent category in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Saksuka or a sufficiently specific Turkish vegetable meze category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Saksuka is approximately 200 g, presented as a shared meze plate or individual side dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11210) and Zucchini, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11478); composite estimate for Saksuka based on standard recipe proportions (eggplant 40%, zucchini 20%, tomato 20%, green pepper 10%, olive oil 8%, garlic/salt 2%)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Saksuka exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients using a standard Turkish Saksuka recipe: eggplant (40%), zucchini (20%), tomato (20%), green pepper (10%), olive oil (8%), garlic and salt (2%). Energy and macros are calculated from weighted component values. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; analogous estimate based on mixed cooked vegetable dish with tomato sauce (GI ~35)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Saksuka. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous food category: mixed cooked non-starchy vegetables with olive oil and tomato sauce. Non-starchy vegetables (eggplant, zucchini, pepper, tomato) have individually low GI values (GI 10–38 per University of Sydney GI Database). Olive oil further attenuates glycemic response. A composite GI of 35 is a conservative defensible estimate. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 6.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 4.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~180 mg/100 g; ~360 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the added salt in Saksuka; a 200 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 360 mg sodium, which is 15–18% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and portion control or requesting reduced salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load (~4.6 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Saksuka is a low-GI dish dominated by non-starchy vegetables and olive oil, producing a glycemic load of approximately 4.6 per 200 g serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat from olive oil; zero cholesterol; rich in dietary fibre and antioxidant vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saksuka's olive oil base provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids (~3.4 g/100 g), and its combination of eggplant, zucchini, tomato, and pepper supplies dietary fibre and antioxidants consistent with a Mediterranean-style dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; sodium intake should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saksuka (Turkish: Şakşuka) is a traditional Turkish meze and side dish consisting of deep-fried or sautéed vegetables — primarily eggplant (aubergine) and zucchini (courgette) — combined with green peppers, tomatoes, and garlic, all brought together in a light tomato-based sauce enriched with olive oil. The dish is served warm or at room temperature and is a staple of Turkish meze spreads. The texture is soft and yielding from the cooked eggplant, with a slightly tangy and savory flavor profile from the tomato and garlic. Saksuka is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal, making it broadly accessible across dietary preferences. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI dish (~72 kcal/100 g, GI ~35) with a favorable fat profile dominated by monounsaturated fats from olive oil. It provides dietary fibre (~2.1 g/100 g), vitamin C (~18 mg/100 g), and vitamin A (~28 mcg RAE/100 g) from its vegetable base. The sodium content (~180 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. In Singapore, Saksuka is served at Turkish restaurants such as Sultan Palace at Boat Quay as part of a meze selection, typically priced around SGD 13.90. It is clinically suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic load and for those following heart-healthy Mediterranean-style diets.
common_user_queries: Is Saksuka the same as Shakshuka?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Saksuka exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component ingredients using standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for mixed cooked non-starchy vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for eggplant, zucchini, tomato, green pepper, and olive oil using a standard Saksuka recipe weight distribution. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — olive oil and plant-based ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients are present). vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy composite used for this estimate. Note: Saksuka (Şakşuka) is distinct from Shakshuka (eggs poached in tomato sauce); they share a similar name but are different dishes. Multilingual coverage: Turkish cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean mandatory language rules (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); those fields are null as not applicable. Chinese-script transliterations are provided as retrieval aids. field survey source data preserved: restaurant_count=2, Sultan Palace Boat Quay, price SGD 13.90.
